title: Cinnamon-swirl coffee cake
subtitle: An American streusel cake with a ribbon of cinnamon through the middle
excerpt: This American-style coffee cake is nothing like a cake with coffee in it: it is a tender soured-cream sponge with a thick cinnamon-brown-sugar swirl running through the centre and a crumbly streusel topping. Served warm, in squares, alongside coffee.
sourceNotes: American coffee cake is documented in home economics literature from the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, including USDA nutrition bulletins from the 1910s (public-domain). The soured-cream streusel version became widespread in American baking by the mid-twentieth century; the form here follows the standard home-baking tradition.

recipe.yieldDescription: One 23 cm square cake (12 to 16 squares)
recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, cut into squares, wrap individually, and freeze. Thaw at room temperature for 45 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well at room temperature for 3 days, covered. The streusel stays crisp if kept uncovered in a cool kitchen.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 190°C; check at 35 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A crumbly topping made by rubbing cold butter into flour and sugar (and often spices) until the mixture forms clumps the size of peas. It is scattered over the top of a cake or tart before baking and bakes to a crisp, sandy texture. The word is German, meaning 'strewn'.

content.text: American coffee cake has no coffee in it. The name refers to the occasion rather than an ingredient: this is the cake you cut when the coffee is made, the one that sits in a square tin on the kitchen counter at 10 in the morning. It is a soured-cream sponge with a cinnamon-brown-sugar swirl running through the centre and a

topping that bakes to a crumble. It is not complicated. The batter is assembled in one bowl; the swirl is a separate mixture scattered over the middle layer of batter. The whole thing takes 25 minutes to put together and 40 minutes to bake.

Combine the streusel flour, brown sugar, and cinnamon in a medium bowl. Add the cold cubed butter and rub it in with your fingertips until the mixture forms irregular clumps the size of peas. Do not over-rub; some dry streaks are fine. Refrigerate until needed.

Preheat the oven to 175°C (fan). Grease and line a 23 cm square cake tin. In a separate small bowl, mix the dark brown sugar and cinnamon for the swirl. Set aside. Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes with an electric mixer or 5 minutes by hand. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each addition. Add the vanilla. Fold in the flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, and salt alternately with the soured cream, beginning and ending with the flour, in three additions of flour and two of soured cream. Do not over-mix; stop when streaks of flour have just disappeared.

Spoon half the batter into the prepared tin and spread level. Scatter the cinnamon-brown-sugar swirl mixture evenly over the surface. Spoon the remaining batter over the top and spread gently; it does not need to be perfectly even. Scatter the cold streusel over the top. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The edges will be pulling from the tin and the streusel will be set and lightly crisped. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then cut into squares and serve warm, or transfer to a rack.
